weeks.title: Week 1 — Spring Boot setup + first REST endpoints
weeks.description: Get your first Spring Boot app running and understand how the framework is structured.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Create your first Spring Boot project

tasks.text: Go to start.spring.io — choose Maven, Java 21, Spring Boot 3.x (latest stable)
tasks.description: Spring Initializr generates the project skeleton so you don't have to configure Maven and the folder structure by hand.

tasks.text: Add dependencies: Spring Web, Spring Data JPA, MySQL Driver, Lombok
tasks.description: These four dependencies are the minimum needed to build a database-backed REST API.

tasks.text: Open the project in IntelliJ — explore the folder structure
tasks.description: Knowing where controllers, config, and resources live makes the project much easier to navigate as it grows.

tasks.text: Understand: what is @SpringBootApplication and the main() method
tasks.description: @SpringBootApplication bootstraps auto-configuration, component scanning, and the embedded server in one annotation.

tasks.text: Configure application.properties: datasource URL, username, password, ddl-auto=update
tasks.description: These properties tell Spring how to connect to your MySQL database and whether to auto-create tables.

tasks.text: Run the app — confirm it starts without errors in the console
tasks.description: A clean startup confirms your dependencies, database connection, and configuration are all wired correctly before you write any code.

days.resource: Note: Spring Boot 3.x uses Jakarta EE (not javax). When you see import errors, use jakarta.* not javax.*

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: First REST controller

tasks.text: Learn: @RestController vs @Controller — what's the difference
tasks.description: @RestController combines @Controller and @ResponseBody — it tells Spring to return data (JSON) instead of view templates.

tasks.text: Learn: @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, @DeleteMapping
tasks.description: These mapping annotations tie HTTP verbs to Java methods — the core of how REST endpoints are defined.

tasks.text: Learn: @RequestMapping for base path on a controller class
tasks.description: @RequestMapping on the class lets you avoid repeating the same path prefix on every method.

tasks.text: Build: HelloController with GET /api/hello returning a String
tasks.description: Your first endpoint — proves the controller, mapping, and embedded server all work together.

tasks.text: Test: open browser or Postman, hit http://localhost:8080/api/hello
tasks.description: Testing in the browser/Postman confirms your endpoint is reachable and returns what you expect.

tasks.text: Install Postman and create a collection called "Product API"
tasks.description: Postman is how you'll test every endpoint you build — a collection keeps your requests organized as the API grows.

days.resource: Resource: Dan Vega on YouTube — "Building REST APIs with Spring Boot" (free, 2024 version)

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Request handling — params, path vars, body

tasks.text: Learn: @PathVariable — GET /api/products/{id}
tasks.description: @PathVariable lets a URL segment become a method parameter — used for fetching a specific resource by ID.

tasks.text: Learn: @RequestParam — GET /api/products?page=0&size=10
tasks.description: @RequestParam reads query string values — used for filtering, paging, and search.

tasks.text: Learn: @RequestBody — receive JSON in POST/PUT requests
tasks.description: @RequestBody deserializes incoming JSON into a Java object — how clients send data to create or update resources.

tasks.text: Learn: ResponseEntity — control HTTP status codes in your response
tasks.description: ResponseEntity lets you control the status code and headers, not just the body — needed for proper REST responses.

tasks.text: Build: ProductController with in-memory List (no DB yet) — GET all, GET by id, POST, DELETE
tasks.description: Building a full CRUD controller with an in-memory list practices all four request-handling annotations together before adding a database.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Layered architecture — Service layer

tasks.text: Learn: why separate Controller, Service, Repository layers
tasks.description: Separating layers keeps each class focused on one concern, making the codebase easier to test and change.

tasks.text: Learn: @Service annotation and Dependency Injection via constructor
tasks.description: @Service marks a class as business logic, and constructor injection is how Spring wires dependencies together.

tasks.text: Learn: how Spring's IoC container manages beans automatically
tasks.description: Understanding the IoC container demystifies how @Service and @Repository beans get created and injected automatically.

tasks.text: Refactor: move business logic out of controller into ProductService
tasks.description: Moving logic out of the controller is the first step toward the Controller → Service → Repository structure used throughout the curriculum.

tasks.text: Inject ProductService into ProductController using constructor injection
tasks.description: Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit and testable — the standard way to wire a Service into a Controller.

days.resource: Rule to remember: Controller talks to Service. Service talks to Repository. Repository talks to DB. Never skip a layer.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Exception handling

tasks.text: Learn: what happens when your controller throws an unhandled exception
tasks.description: Seeing the default error response (a stack trace dump) shows why custom exception handling is necessary.

tasks.text: Learn: @ControllerAdvice and @ExceptionHandler
tasks.description: @ControllerAdvice + @ExceptionHandler centralizes error handling so you don't repeat try-catch in every controller.

tasks.text: Build: a custom ResourceNotFoundException class
tasks.description: A custom exception lets you signal a specific error condition (like 'not found') that the global handler can catch and format.

tasks.text: Build: a GlobalExceptionHandler that returns JSON error responses
tasks.description: The GlobalExceptionHandler turns exceptions into consistent JSON error responses your frontend can rely on.

tasks.text: Test: request a product by a non-existent ID — confirm clean 404 JSON response
tasks.description: Confirming a clean 404 JSON response (instead of a stack trace) proves your exception handling pipeline works end-to-end.

days.resource: Resource: Amigoscode "Spring Boot Exception Handling" on YouTube

weeks.askPrompt: Show me the exact folder structure and code for a Spring Boot 3 project with Controller, Service, and a GlobalExceptionHandler — beginner-friendly with comments

weeks.title: Week 2 — Spring Data JPA + MySQL
weeks.description: Replace your in-memory list with a real database. This is where it gets exciting.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: JPA entities and the database layer

tasks.text: Learn: what JPA is — a standard for mapping Java objects to DB tables
tasks.description: JPA is what lets you work with Java objects instead of writing raw SQL for every operation.

tasks.text: Learn: @Entity, @Table, @Id, @GeneratedValue(strategy=IDENTITY)
tasks.description: These annotations map a Java class to a database table and define its primary key generation strategy.

tasks.text: Learn: @Column — nullable, unique, length constraints
tasks.description: @Column lets you enforce constraints like NOT NULL and length limits directly from your entity definition.

tasks.text: Learn: Lombok @Data, @NoArgsConstructor, @AllArgsConstructor to cut boilerplate
tasks.description: Lombok generates getters, setters, and constructors for you — removing repetitive boilerplate from every entity.

tasks.text: Build: Product entity (id, name, description, price, stock, createdAt)
tasks.description: This is your first real entity — every field maps directly to a column in the products table.

tasks.text: Run app — verify MySQL Workbench shows the products table was auto-created
tasks.description: Seeing the table auto-created in MySQL confirms JPA successfully translated your entity into a schema.

days.resource: Note: Use ddl-auto=update in dev only. Never in production — use Flyway (covered in Phase 5).

tasks.text: Learn: JpaRepository<Entity, IdType> — what methods you get for free
tasks.description: JpaRepository gives you save, findById, findAll, deleteById, and more — without writing any SQL.

tasks.text: Build: ProductRepository interface that extends JpaRepository
tasks.description: Extending JpaRepository is all it takes to get a fully working data access layer for an entity.

tasks.text: Update ProductService to use ProductRepository (inject it via constructor)
tasks.description: Swapping the in-memory list for a repository connects your service layer to the real database.

tasks.text: Test: POST a product in Postman — confirm it saves to MySQL
tasks.description: Confirming a POST request persists to MySQL proves the full Controller → Service → Repository → DB chain works.

tasks.text: Test: GET /api/products — confirm it returns from DB
tasks.description: Confirming GET reads from the database (not memory) verifies your repository wiring is correct.

tasks.text: Test: DELETE by id — confirm row removed in MySQL Workbench
tasks.description: Confirming DELETE removes the row in MySQL closes the loop on basic CRUD.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: Custom queries + pagination

tasks.text: Learn: derived query methods — findByName(), findByPriceLessThan()
tasks.description: Derived query methods let Spring generate SQL from a method name — no @Query needed for simple lookups.

tasks.text: Learn: @Query annotation with JPQL for custom queries
tasks.description: @Query with JPQL gives you full control for queries that derived methods can't express.

tasks.text: Learn: Pageable and Page<T> for paginated results
tasks.description: Pageable and Page<T> let your API return data in pages instead of dumping the entire table at once.

tasks.text: Build: GET /api/products?page=0&size=5&sort=price,asc — paginated endpoint
tasks.description: Pagination and sorting on a list endpoint is a must-have for any API with more than a handful of rows.

tasks.text: Build: GET /api/products/search?name=laptop — search by name
tasks.description: A search endpoint demonstrates a derived query method (or @Query) used in a real feature.

days.resource: Resource: Spring Data JPA docs — "Query Methods" section at docs.spring.io

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Entity relationships — @OneToMany

tasks.text: Learn: @ManyToOne and @OneToMany — a Category has many Products
tasks.description: @ManyToOne/@OneToMany model real-world relationships — the basis of almost every database schema.

tasks.text: Learn: @JoinColumn — specifies the foreign key column
tasks.description: @JoinColumn tells JPA exactly which column holds the foreign key.

tasks.text: Learn: FetchType.LAZY vs EAGER — always use LAZY, fetch explicitly when needed
tasks.description: LAZY loading avoids fetching related data you don't need — EAGER can silently load huge amounts of data.

tasks.text: Learn: cascade types — when saving parent also saves children
tasks.description: Cascade types control whether saving/deleting a parent automatically affects its children.

tasks.text: Build: Category entity with @OneToMany to Product
tasks.description: Building Category with @OneToMany to Product is your first real one-to-many relationship.

tasks.text: Test: create a category, assign products to it, query products by category
tasks.description: Querying products by category proves the relationship is correctly mapped and navigable.

days.resource: Warning: Never use FetchType.EAGER on collections — it causes the N+1 problem and silently destroys performance.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: DTOs — separate your API from your entities

tasks.text: Learn: why you should never return JPA entities directly from controllers
tasks.description: Returning entities directly exposes internal fields and can cause infinite JSON loops on bidirectional relationships.

tasks.text: Learn: DTO (Data Transfer Object) pattern — a plain class for API input/output
tasks.description: DTOs decouple your API contract from your database schema — you can change one without breaking the other.

tasks.text: Build: ProductRequest DTO (for incoming POST/PUT body)
tasks.description: A request DTO defines exactly what a client is allowed to send when creating/updating a resource.

tasks.text: Build: ProductResponse DTO (for outgoing JSON — only expose safe fields)
tasks.description: A response DTO controls exactly what fields are exposed to clients — hiding internal or sensitive data.

tasks.text: Update service to map entity ↔ DTO manually (or use MapStruct)
tasks.description: Mapping between entity and DTO is what actually enforces the separation — manual mapping builds understanding before reaching for MapStruct.

days.resource: Why it matters: Returning raw entities leaks internal fields (like passwords), causes JSON infinite loops on bidirectional relations, and tightly couples your DB schema to your API.

weeks.askPrompt: Explain the JPA N+1 problem to me with a simple example and show me how to fix it using @EntityGraph or JOIN FETCH in Spring Boot

weeks.title: Week 3 — Validation + advanced JPA
weeks.description: Make your API bulletproof — reject bad data before it touches the database.

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-validation
tasks.description: This starter brings in the Jakarta Bean Validation annotations used to validate DTO fields.

tasks.text: Learn: @NotNull, @NotBlank, @Size, @Min, @Max, @Email on DTO fields
tasks.description: These annotations declaratively enforce rules like required fields, length limits, and valid email format.

tasks.text: Learn: add @Valid to @RequestBody parameter in controller
tasks.description: @Valid tells Spring to run validation on the incoming body before your controller method executes.

tasks.text: Learn: handle MethodArgumentNotValidException in GlobalExceptionHandler
tasks.description: Handling MethodArgumentNotValidException lets you turn validation failures into a clean 400 response instead of a 500.

tasks.text: Test: POST a product with a blank name — confirm 400 with helpful error message
tasks.description: Testing with invalid input confirms your validation rules and error handling actually work.

tasks.text: Build: return a map of field → error message in validation failure response
tasks.description: A field-to-message map gives the frontend exactly what it needs to show inline form errors.

tasks.text: Learn: @ManyToMany — a Product can have many Tags, a Tag can have many Products
tasks.description: @ManyToMany models relationships where both sides can have multiple matches — like products and tags.

tasks.text: Learn: @JoinTable — defines the join table (product_tags) with both foreign keys
tasks.description: @JoinTable defines the hidden join table that stores the many-to-many links.

tasks.text: Learn: owning side vs inverse side of the relationship
tasks.description: Knowing which side owns the relationship determines which entity controls saving the join table rows.

tasks.text: Build: Tag entity + @ManyToMany on Product
tasks.description: Building Tag with @ManyToMany to Product gives you hands-on experience with the trickiest relationship type.

tasks.text: Test: create tags, assign to products, query products by tag
tasks.description: Querying products by tag confirms the many-to-many mapping and join table are working correctly.

tasks.text: Learn: @EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class)
tasks.description: @EntityListeners hooks JPA lifecycle events so audit fields update automatically without manual code.

tasks.text: Learn: @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate on timestamp fields
tasks.description: @CreatedDate and @LastModifiedDate are populated automatically by Spring Data JPA auditing.

tasks.text: Learn: @EnableJpaAuditing on your main class or a config class
tasks.description: @EnableJpaAuditing turns on the auditing infrastructure for your whole application.

tasks.text: Build: an abstract BaseEntity class with audit fields — all entities extend it
tasks.description: A shared BaseEntity avoids repeating audit fields on every entity class.

tasks.text: Test: create a product, confirm createdAt and updatedAt auto-populate
tasks.description: Confirming timestamps auto-populate verifies your auditing setup is wired correctly.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Service layer patterns

tasks.text: Learn: @Transactional — when and why to add it to service methods
tasks.description: @Transactional ensures multi-step database operations either all succeed or all roll back together.

tasks.text: Learn: readOnly=true for GET service methods — performance hint to JPA
tasks.description: readOnly=true lets JPA skip dirty-checking overhead for queries that don't modify data.

tasks.text: Learn: how to do a partial update (PATCH) — only update non-null fields
tasks.description: Partial updates let clients change just one field without resending the entire resource.

tasks.text: Build: PATCH /api/products/{id} endpoint with partial update logic
tasks.description: PATCH is the correct HTTP verb for partial updates — building it teaches the null-check update pattern.

tasks.text: Test: PATCH with only price changed — confirm name untouched in DB
tasks.description: Confirming untouched fields stay the same proves your partial update logic doesn't overwrite with nulls.

tasks.text: Learn: @EnableCaching, @Cacheable("products") on getAllProducts() — add @CacheEvict on create/update/delete so the cache stays fresh
tasks.description: Caching avoids hitting the database for data that rarely changes, while cache eviction keeps it from going stale after writes.

days.resource: Pattern: For PATCH, fetch entity from DB, check each DTO field for null, update only non-null fields, save. Never blindly overwrite.

tasks.text: Learn: RESTful URL conventions — nouns not verbs (/products not /getProducts)
tasks.description: Consistent, noun-based URLs make your API predictable and easier for frontend developers to use.

tasks.text: Learn: correct HTTP status codes — 200, 201, 204, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 500
tasks.description: Correct status codes communicate the outcome of a request precisely — clients rely on them to handle responses correctly.

tasks.text: Learn: consistent error response format — include status, message, timestamp, path
tasks.description: A consistent error shape means the frontend can handle all errors with one piece of code.

tasks.text: Learn: API versioning with URL prefix — /api/v1/products
tasks.description: Versioning your API lets you make breaking changes later without breaking existing clients.

tasks.text: Refactor: update all your endpoints to follow these conventions
tasks.description: Refactoring existing endpoints to follow these conventions makes your whole API consistent before moving to security.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to implement @Transactional correctly in a Spring Boot service layer — with a real example of when it matters and what happens without it

weeks.title: Week 4 — Spring Security + JWT authentication
weeks.description: The hardest week. Take it slow — Security trips up every beginner. Understand each step before moving on.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Spring Security basics

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-security
tasks.description: This starter adds Spring Security to your project and locks down all endpoints by default.

tasks.text: Observe: all your endpoints are now locked behind HTTP Basic auth
tasks.description: Seeing every endpoint suddenly require a login shows Spring Security's secure-by-default behavior.

tasks.text: Learn: the Security Filter Chain concept — requests go through filters before hitting controllers
tasks.description: Understanding the filter chain explains how every request is checked for authentication before reaching your controller.

tasks.text: Learn: @EnableWebSecurity and writing a SecurityFilterChain @Bean
tasks.description: A custom SecurityFilterChain bean is how you configure which routes require authentication and how.

tasks.text: Learn: how to permit certain URLs (e.g. /api/v1/auth/**) and require auth on others
tasks.description: Permitting auth endpoints while securing the rest is the basic shape of every secured API.

tasks.text: Build: User entity with email, password, role fields
tasks.description: The User entity is the foundation everything else this week builds on — registration, login, and roles all depend on it.

days.resource: Resource: Amigoscode "Spring Boot 3 + Spring Security 6 + JWT" on YouTube — watch Day 1 portion only today

tasks.text: Learn: UserDetails interface — what Spring Security expects from a user object
tasks.description: UserDetails is the contract Spring Security uses to represent an authenticated user.

tasks.text: Make your User entity implement UserDetails (or wrap it in a separate class)
tasks.description: Implementing UserDetails lets your User entity plug directly into Spring Security's authentication process.

tasks.text: Learn: UserDetailsService — loadUserByUsername() method Spring calls during login
tasks.description: loadUserByUsername() is the hook Spring Security calls to find a user during login.

tasks.text: Build: UserDetailsServiceImpl that loads user from DB by email
tasks.description: This service connects your database to Spring Security's authentication mechanism.

tasks.text: Learn: BCryptPasswordEncoder — never store plain text passwords
tasks.description: BCrypt hashes passwords one-way so even a database breach doesn't expose plain-text passwords.

tasks.text: Register PasswordEncoder @Bean — hash password on registration, verify on login
tasks.description: Hashing on registration and verifying on login is the only safe way to handle passwords.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: JWT token generation

tasks.text: Add dependency: io.jsonwebtoken:jjwt-api, jjwt-impl, jjwt-jackson
tasks.description: These libraries provide the tools to create and verify JWT tokens in Java.

tasks.text: Learn: JWT structure — header.payload.signature
tasks.description: Understanding JWT structure helps you debug auth issues by inspecting tokens directly.

tasks.text: Learn: what goes in the claims — subject (email), issued at, expiration
tasks.description: Claims carry the user identity and expiration inside the token itself — no server-side session needed.

tasks.text: Build: JwtService with generateToken() and extractUsername() methods
tasks.description: JwtService centralizes all token creation and parsing logic in one place.

tasks.text: Store JWT secret and expiration in application.properties (externalized)
tasks.description: Externalizing the secret and expiration keeps sensitive config out of your source code.

tasks.text: Test: generate a token, paste into jwt.io to inspect the claims
tasks.description: Inspecting a generated token on jwt.io confirms the claims and structure are correct.

days.resource: Resource: jwt.io — paste any JWT token here to decode and inspect it visually

tasks.text: Build: JwtAuthenticationFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter
tasks.description: A custom filter is how every incoming request gets checked for a valid JWT before reaching your controllers.

tasks.text: In filter: read Authorization header, extract Bearer token, validate, set SecurityContext
tasks.description: This is the core logic that turns a Bearer token into an authenticated SecurityContext for the request.

tasks.text: Register the filter before UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter in SecurityFilterChain
tasks.description: Registering the filter in the right order ensures JWT validation happens before Spring's default authentication.

tasks.text: Build: AuthController with POST /api/v1/auth/register and /login
tasks.description: Register and login endpoints are the entry points users interact with to obtain a token.

tasks.text: Build: AuthService — register saves hashed password, login verifies and returns JWT
tasks.description: AuthService ties together password hashing, user lookup, and token generation into the registration/login flow.

tasks.text: Learn: roles — USER vs ADMIN — store as enum in User entity
tasks.description: Roles let you distinguish what different users are allowed to do — e.g. only admins can manage products.

tasks.text: Learn: .hasRole() and .hasAuthority() in SecurityFilterChain
tasks.description: These methods restrict access to endpoints based on a user's role or authority.

tasks.text: Learn: @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") on controller methods
tasks.description: @PreAuthorize lets you enforce role checks at the method level, closer to the business logic.

tasks.text: Configure CORS in SecurityFilterChain to allow your frontend origin
tasks.description: CORS configuration is required for your React frontend (running on a different origin) to call this API.

tasks.text: Test full flow in Postman: register → login → get token → use token on protected endpoint
tasks.description: This end-to-end test proves the entire authentication flow works from the client's perspective.

tasks.text: Build: GET /api/v1/auth/me — use @AuthenticationPrincipal UserDetails to return the current user as a UserResponse DTO (never the password)
tasks.description: /auth/me is how the frontend finds out who's currently logged in — a common requirement for any authenticated app.

weeks.askPrompt: Walk me through building a complete JWT authentication system in Spring Boot 3 step by step — from User entity to JwtAuthenticationFilter — with code for each class

weeks.title: Week 5 — File uploads + email + testing
weeks.description: Round out your API with real-world features and make sure it works correctly.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Multipart file upload

tasks.text: Learn: @RequestParam MultipartFile file in controller
tasks.description: MultipartFile is how Spring represents an uploaded file in a controller method.

tasks.text: Learn: file.getOriginalFilename(), file.getBytes(), file.getInputStream()
tasks.description: These methods give you access to the file's name and contents so you can save or process it.

tasks.text: Configure: spring.servlet.multipart.max-file-size in application.properties
tasks.description: Setting a max file size prevents users from uploading files that could overwhelm your server.

tasks.text: Build: POST /api/v1/products/{id}/image — saves file to local /uploads/ folder
tasks.description: A real upload endpoint is needed for features like product images — a common e-commerce requirement.

tasks.text: Build: GET endpoint to serve the uploaded image back
tasks.description: Serving the uploaded file back lets the frontend display the image after upload.

tasks.text: Test: upload a product image via Postman form-data
tasks.description: Testing with form-data confirms the upload endpoint works the way a real frontend would call it.

days.resource: Next step: In Phase 4 you'll swap local storage for Cloudflare R2 or AWS S3 — same API, different storage backend.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Sending emails with JavaMailSender

tasks.text: Add dependency: spring-boot-starter-mail
tasks.description: This starter provides JavaMailSender for sending emails from your application.

tasks.text: Configure: SMTP settings in application.properties (use Mailtrap.io for testing)
tasks.description: SMTP configuration tells Spring where to send emails — Mailtrap is safe for testing without spamming real inboxes.

tasks.text: Build: EmailService with sendSimpleEmail() method using JavaMailSender
tasks.description: EmailService centralizes email-sending logic so it can be reused for welcome emails, password resets, etc.

tasks.text: Trigger: send welcome email on user registration
tasks.description: A welcome email on registration is a common real-world feature that ties together your auth and email systems.

tasks.text: Test: register a user, check Mailtrap inbox for the email
tasks.description: Checking Mailtrap confirms the email was actually sent and formatted correctly.

days.resource: Resource: mailtrap.io — free fake SMTP inbox for dev/testing. No real emails sent.

tasks.text: Learn: what a unit test is — test one class in isolation
tasks.description: Unit tests catch bugs in your business logic without needing a database or running server.

tasks.text: Learn: @Test, @BeforeEach, Assertions.assertEquals(), assertThrows()
tasks.description: These JUnit annotations and assertions are the basic vocabulary of writing any test.

tasks.text: Learn: Mockito — @Mock, @InjectMocks, when().thenReturn(), verify()
tasks.description: Mockito lets you fake dependencies like repositories so you can test a service in isolation.

tasks.text: Write: unit tests for ProductService — mock the repository, test business logic
tasks.description: Testing ProductService with a mocked repository verifies your business logic independent of the database.

tasks.text: Write: test the "product not found" exception is thrown correctly
tasks.description: Testing exception scenarios ensures your error handling behaves correctly, not just the happy path.

tasks.text: Run tests — aim for green on all service tests
tasks.description: A green test suite gives you confidence to refactor without breaking existing behavior.

tasks.text: Learn: @WebMvcTest — loads only web layer, mocks everything else
tasks.description: @WebMvcTest is faster than a full integration test because it only loads the web layer.

tasks.text: Learn: MockMvc — perform HTTP requests in tests without a running server
tasks.description: MockMvc simulates HTTP requests so you can test controllers without starting a real server.

tasks.text: Write: test GET /api/v1/products returns 200 with correct JSON body
tasks.description: Testing a successful GET confirms the controller, JSON serialization, and status code all work together.

tasks.text: Write: test POST with invalid body returns 400 with validation errors
tasks.description: Testing invalid input confirms your validation and error handling work at the HTTP layer.

tasks.text: Write: test authenticated endpoint returns 401 without JWT
tasks.description: Testing an unauthenticated request confirms your security configuration actually protects the endpoint.

tasks.text: Add H2 (test scope) + application-test.properties (ddl-auto=create-drop) — write a @SpringBootTest integration test for the full register → login → use-token flow
tasks.description: An end-to-end integration test with H2 verifies the whole register → login → authenticated request flow works together.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Logging + environment config

tasks.text: Learn: SLF4J with Logback — @Slf4j (Lombok) and log.info(), log.error(), log.debug()
tasks.description: Proper logging gives you visibility into what your app is doing in production, unlike println which can't be filtered or disabled.

tasks.text: Add meaningful log statements to service methods (not System.out.println!)
tasks.description: Meaningful logs make debugging production issues possible without redeploying with print statements.

tasks.text: Learn: Spring profiles — application-dev.properties vs application-prod.properties
tasks.description: Profiles let you use different configuration for local development vs production without code changes.

tasks.text: Move DB credentials and JWT secret to environment variables
tasks.description: Environment variables keep secrets out of your codebase and let different environments use different credentials.

tasks.text: Never commit secrets to Git — add application-prod.properties to .gitignore
tasks.description: Committing secrets to Git exposes them permanently in history, even if removed later — .gitignore prevents this mistake.

days.resource: Rule: If it's a secret (password, API key, JWT secret), it lives in an environment variable — never in code or committed properties files.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to write a JUnit 5 unit test for a Spring Boot service class using Mockito — with @Mock, @InjectMocks, and testing an exception scenario

weeks.title: Week 6 — Polish, Postman docs + deployment prep
weeks.description: Make your API production-ready and deploy it so the world can see it.

tasks.text: Add dependency: springdoc-openapi-starter-webmvc-ui
tasks.description: Springdoc generates interactive API documentation automatically from your existing controllers and DTOs.

tasks.text: Visit http://localhost:8080/swagger-ui.html — your API is auto-documented
tasks.description: Swagger UI gives you (and anyone else) a browsable, testable view of your entire API.

tasks.text: Learn: @Operation, @Parameter, @ApiResponse annotations for richer docs
tasks.description: These annotations let you add descriptions and examples that make the auto-generated docs more useful.

tasks.text: Configure Swagger to support JWT — add Authorize button for Bearer token
tasks.description: Adding JWT support to Swagger lets you test protected endpoints directly from the docs UI.

tasks.text: Test: use Swagger UI to call your endpoints — confirm auth works through UI
tasks.description: Confirming auth works through Swagger means your docs are a fully functional testing tool, not just a reference.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Deploy to Railway

tasks.text: Create Railway account at railway.app
tasks.description: Railway is a simple, low-cost platform to host both your app and database for this project.

tasks.text: Add a MySQL service to your Railway project
tasks.description: A managed MySQL service on Railway gives your deployed app a real database to connect to.

tasks.text: Connect your GitHub repo to Railway — enable auto-deploy on push to main
tasks.description: Auto-deploy on push means every commit to main automatically updates your live API.

tasks.text: Set environment variables in Railway dashboard (DB_URL, DB_USER, DB_PASS, JWT_SECRET)
tasks.description: Environment variables on Railway keep your production secrets separate from your code, just like locally.

tasks.text: Deploy — watch the Railway build logs, fix any startup errors
tasks.description: Watching the build logs is how you'll catch and fix deployment-specific issues that don't show up locally.

tasks.text: Test your live API URL in Postman — full register/login/CRUD flow
tasks.description: Testing the live URL confirms your app actually works in production, not just on your machine.

days.resource: Tip: Railway gives you a public URL like yourapp.up.railway.app — save this, you'll connect your React frontend to it in Phase 4.

tasks.text: Organise all your requests into folders in Postman (Auth, Products, Categories)
tasks.description: Organizing requests into folders makes a large API collection navigable for you and anyone reviewing your work.

tasks.text: Create a Postman environment with {{baseUrl}} and {{token}} variables
tasks.description: Environment variables in Postman let you switch between local and live URLs without editing every request.

tasks.text: Add a Postman test script on the login request to auto-save token to environment
tasks.description: Auto-saving the token after login means you don't have to manually copy/paste it for every authenticated request.

tasks.text: Add request examples (sample bodies) to every endpoint in the collection
tasks.description: Example request bodies make the collection self-documenting for anyone who opens it.

tasks.text: Export collection and commit it to GitHub in a /postman folder
tasks.description: A committed Postman collection lets reviewers (or future you) test the API without writing requests from scratch.

tasks.text: Review each class — does every method do exactly one thing?
tasks.description: Methods that do one thing are easier to test, debug, and reuse — a core principle of clean code.

tasks.text: Remove all System.out.println() — replace with proper log statements
tasks.description: println statements don't appear in production logs and can't be filtered — proper logging is a must before going live.

tasks.text: Check all endpoints return the right HTTP status code (POST returns 201, DELETE returns 204)
tasks.description: Correct status codes are part of the REST contract — clients and tools depend on them being accurate.

tasks.text: Ensure no entity is ever returned directly — only DTOs
tasks.description: A final check that no entity leaks through an endpoint closes any remaining security/coupling gaps.

tasks.text: Write a README.md with project setup steps, env vars needed, and API endpoint list
tasks.description: A good README is often the first thing anyone (including employers) looks at when evaluating your project.

tasks.text: Run your full Postman collection against the live Railway URL — everything green?
tasks.description: Running the full collection against the live URL is your final proof that the deployed API works end-to-end.

tasks.text: Note your live API base URL — you'll need it in Phase 3
tasks.description: You'll need this URL in Phase 3 to connect your React frontend to this backend.

tasks.text: Install Node.js and npm — required to start Phase 3 (React)
tasks.description: Node.js and npm are required tooling for every React project starting in Phase 3.

tasks.text: Scaffold a Vite React project: npm create vite@latest — just to preview what's coming
tasks.description: Scaffolding a Vite project now gives you a preview of the tooling and structure you'll be using next.

tasks.text: Push everything to GitHub — backend repo should be clean and well-documented
tasks.description: A clean, documented backend repo is the foundation of your portfolio — make sure it's presentable.

tasks.text: Self-assessment: without notes, draw the layered architecture (Controller → Service → Repository → DB), explain how JWT auth flows through the filter chain, and write a JPA entity with a @ManyToOne relationship from memory
tasks.description: This self-assessment confirms you understand the architecture and security flow well enough to build on it in Phase 3.

weeks.askPrompt: How do I set up Springdoc OpenAPI Swagger UI in Spring Boot 3 with JWT authorization support — show me the config and dependency

weeks.title: Phase 2 project — Product Catalog REST API
weeks.description: Your complete backend, tested in Postman and deployed live on Railway.

projectCards.heading: What you'll build
projectCards.text: A production-ready REST API for an e-commerce product catalog with user registration, JWT login, role-based access, full product and category CRUD, image uploads, search, pagination, and live deployment.

projectCards.heading: Entities
projectCards.text: User (id, name, email, password, role) · Category (id, name, slug) · Product (id, name, description, price, stock, imageUrl, category, createdAt, updatedAt) · Tag (id, name) · Product_Tags join table

tasks.text: POST /api/v1/auth/register — create user account
tasks.description: Registration is the entry point for every user — without it, nothing else in the auth flow can work.

tasks.text: POST /api/v1/auth/login — returns JWT token
tasks.description: Login is what issues the JWT that protects every other endpoint.

tasks.text: GET /api/v1/auth/me — returns current user from token
tasks.description: This endpoint lets the frontend display who's logged in and adjust the UI based on their role.

tasks.text: GET /api/v1/products — paginated, public
tasks.description: Pagination is essential for a product catalog — clients shouldn't have to load every product at once.

tasks.text: GET /api/v1/products/{id} — public
tasks.description: A single-product endpoint is needed for product detail pages.

tasks.text: GET /api/v1/products/search?name= — public
tasks.description: Search is a baseline e-commerce feature users expect.

tasks.text: POST /api/v1/products — ADMIN only
tasks.description: Restricting product creation to admins protects your catalog from unauthorized changes.

tasks.text: PUT /api/v1/products/{id} — ADMIN only
tasks.description: Restricting updates to admins keeps product data consistent and trustworthy.

tasks.text: DELETE /api/v1/products/{id} — ADMIN only
tasks.description: Restricting deletes to admins prevents accidental or malicious data loss.

tasks.text: POST /api/v1/products/{id}/image — upload product image
tasks.description: Image uploads make the catalog visually usable — a product without an image is hard to sell.

tasks.text: CRUD for /api/v1/categories
tasks.description: Categories let users browse and filter products in a structured way.

tasks.text: Swagger UI live at /swagger-ui.html
tasks.description: Live Swagger docs make your API self-explanatory for anyone integrating with it, including your future React frontend.

tasks.text: Deployed and accessible on Railway public URL
tasks.description: A live deployment is what makes this a real, demonstrable project rather than just local code.

tasks.text: GET /api/v1/products cached with @Cacheable, evicted on create/update/delete
tasks.description: Caching the product list reduces database load for your most frequently hit endpoint.

tasks.text: Integration test (H2 + @SpringBootTest) covering register → login → use-token flow
tasks.description: An integration test covering the full auth flow gives you confidence the deployed app actually works as a whole.

weeks.tip: A live, documented REST API with JWT auth is a strong portfolio piece on its own. When this is done, you have the backend every React frontend in Phase 3 will connect to.
weeks.askPrompt: Help me start building the Phase 2 project — a Product Catalog REST API. Begin with the User entity, AuthController, and JWT setup in Spring Boot 3.
